elements.sent-2411-001: The rapid advancement of artificial intelligence has led to increasingly sophisticated deep learning models, which frequently operate as opaque ``black boxes'' with limited transparency in their decision-making processes.
elements.sent-2411-002: This lack of interpretability presents considerable challenges, especially in high-stakes applications where understanding the rationale behind a model’s outputs is as essential as the outputs themselves.
elements.sent-2411-003: This study addresses the pressing need for interpretability in AI systems, emphasizing its role in fostering trust, ensuring accountability, and promoting responsible deployment in mission-critical fields.
elements.sent-2411-004: To address the interpretability challenge in deep learning, we introduce DLBacktrace, an innovative technique developed by the AryaXAI team to illuminate model decisions across a wide array of domains, including simple Multi Layer Perceptron (MLPs), Convolutional Neural Networks (CNNs), Large Language Models (LLMs), Computer Vision Models, and more.
elements.sent-2411-005: We provide a comprehensive overview of the DLBacktrace algorithm and present benchmarking results, comparing its performance against established interpretability methods, such as SHAP, LIME, GradCAM, Integrated Gradients, SmoothGrad, and Attention Rollout, using diverse task-based metrics.
elements.sent-2411-006: The proposed DLBacktrace technique is compatible with various model architectures built in PyTorch and TensorFlow, supporting models like Llama 3.2, other NLP architectures such as BERT and LSTMs, computer vision models like ResNet and U-Net, as well as custom deep neural network (DNN) models for tabular data.
elements.sent-2411-007: This flexibility underscores DLBacktrace’s adaptability and effectiveness in enhancing model transparency across a broad spectrum of applications.
elements.sent-2411-008: The library is open-sourced and available at \url{https://github.com/AryaXAI/DLBacktrace .}
elements.sent-2411-009: Despite remarkable advancements in artificial intelligence, particularly with the evolution of deep learning architectures, even the most sophisticated models face a persistent challenge: they often operate as "black boxes," with internal processes that remain opaque and difficult to interpret.
elements.sent-2411-010: These models produce highly accurate predictions, yet provide limited insights into how and why they make specific decisions.
elements.sent-2411-011: This opacity raises significant concerns, especially in high-stakes applications like healthcare, finance, and law enforcement, where understanding the rationale behind model outputs is critical.
elements.sent-2411-012: For example, in the healthcare sector, AI-driven diagnostics must be interpretable to ensure that medical professionals can trust and act on recommendations for patient treatment.
elements.sent-2411-013: Similarly, in finance, regulations such as the European Union’s GDPR mandate a "right to explanation" for automated decisions affecting individuals, making explainability not only an ethical imperative but also a regulatory requirement.
elements.sent-2411-014: The growing demand for explainability and transparency in AI systems is often eclipsed by the prevailing focus on maximizing raw performance.
elements.sent-2411-015: This trend is especially evident with the increasing use of models like OpenAI’s ChatGPT\cite{chatgpt , Meta’s LLaMA \cite{Touvron2023LLaMAOA , Google's Gemini, and similar large language models (LLMs) that are widely adopted for tasks such as language generation, classification, and complex question answering.}}
elements.sent-2411-016: These models frequently operate as ‘black boxes,’ offering limited insight into their decision-making processes.
elements.sent-2411-017: Many of their architectures are also proprietary and closed-source, adding to their opacity.
elements.sent-2411-018: However, due to their high accuracy and utility in various applications, their lack of transparency is often accepted by users and stakeholders.
elements.sent-2411-019: Consequently, interpretability is frequently overlooked or considered secondary to model performance in deployment.
elements.sent-2411-020: This disregard can lead to challenges, such as increased uncertainty in responses to out-of-domain questions and potential regulatory concerns.
elements.sent-2411-021: Over the years, various efforts have been made to make machine learning models more transparent, leading to the development of interpretability methods.
elements.sent-2411-022: Popular approaches like Local Interpretable Model-agnostic Explanations (LIME) \cite{Ribeiro2016WhySI and SHapley Additive exPlanations (SHAP) \cite{Lundberg2017AUA aim to clarify model decisions by assigning feature importance scores.}}
elements.sent-2411-023: These methods work well in tabular data contexts but encounter challenges with complex data types such as images and text, where feature interactions and contextual nuances are intricate.
elements.sent-2411-024: Both LIME and SHAP require repeated evaluations and data perturbations to estimate importance scores, which can increase computational load significantly, especially in high-dimensional contexts.
elements.sent-2411-025: They generate explanations by analyzing selected subsets of data, which leads to instance-specific (local) feature importance.
elements.sent-2411-026: This method may hinder their ability to offer a comprehensive, model-wide perspective, impacting both interpretability and reliability.
elements.sent-2411-027: This issue is particularly pronounced when real-time insights are needed, as the time required to generate explanations increases exponentially with the size of the data subset.
elements.sent-2411-028: For complex data like images and text, interpretability methods such as Grad-CAM, Integrated Gradients \cite{Sundararajan2017AxiomaticAF , and SmoothGrad help highlight influential regions or tokens in model predictions but come with limitations: Grad-CAM is restricted to CNNs, Integrated Gradients requires carefully chosen baselines, and SmoothGrad’s perturbations add computational cost.}
elements.sent-2411-029: For transformer-based models like Vision Transformers (ViTs), techniques such as Attention Rollout aggregate attention across layers to trace information flow, and tools like BertViz \cite{vig-2019-multiscale visualize attention heads, providing insights into the model's decision-making.}
elements.sent-2411-030: However, interpreting attention alone can be challenging, as attention weights don’t always correlate directly with feature importance, requiring careful analysis for a fuller understanding.
elements.sent-2411-031: In response to the pressing challenges of interpretability in deep learning, we present, DLBacktrace a model-agnostic method for deep learning interpretability by tracing relevance from output to input, assigning relevance scores across layers to reveal feature importance, information flow, and bias within predictions.
elements.sent-2411-032: Operating independently of auxiliary models or baselines, DLBacktrace ensures deterministic, consistent interpretations across various architectures and data types, including images, text, and tabular data.
elements.sent-2411-033: This approach supports both local (instance-specific) and global (aggregate) analysis, enhancing transparency for models such as LLMs (e.g., BERT, Llama) and computer vision architectures (e.g., ResNet, U-Net), making it a reliable tool for detailed model interpretation and validation.
elements.sent-2411-034: This technique addresses limitations in current interpretability frameworks by delivering stable, reliable explanations that accurately reflect decision-making pathways within a model, with insights at both local (instance-specific) and global (feature-aggregate) levels.
elements.sent-2411-035: In this work, we make the following contributions:.
elements.sent-2411-036: Introduction of DLBacktrace: A detailed methodology outlining the model-agnostic and deterministic approach of DLBacktrace for achieving enhanced interpretability in AI systems.
elements.sent-2411-037: Comprehensive Benchmarking: We benchmark DLBacktrace against widely used interpretability methods (e.g., LIME, SHAP, Grad-CAM, Integrated Gradients and more) across different tasks.
elements.sent-2411-038: Cross-Modality Applications: DLBacktrace's adaptability is illustrated across various data types, including tabular, image, and text, addressing limitations in current interpretability methods within these domains.
elements.sent-2411-039: Framework for Reliable Interpretability: By providing consistent relevance scores, DLBacktrace contributes to more reliable, regulatory-compliant AI systems, supporting ethical and responsible AI deployment.
elements.subsubsection-2411-001: XAI for Responsible and Trustworthy AI
elements.sent-2411-040: Responsible AI is essential for deploying systems that align with ethical standards and societal values, especially in critical sectors like healthcare, finance, and law enforcement, where AI decisions can profoundly impact individuals and communities.
elements.sent-2411-041: Responsible AI emphasizes fairness, transparency, accountability, privacy, and ethical alignment to prevent bias, protect individual rights, and foster public trust.
elements.sent-2411-042: Fairness ensures that AI models do not discriminate and treat individuals equitably, while transparency provides clear explanations of AI decision-making to build trust.
elements.sent-2411-043: Accountability involves defining responsibility for AI outcomes and enabling human oversight.
elements.sent-2411-044: Privacy and security focus on protecting personal data and ensuring system resilience against security threats, and ethical alignment ensures AI development respects human rights and societal norms.
elements.sent-2411-045: There has been substantial progress toward responsible AI, supported by regulatory frameworks like the EU's General Data Protection Regulation (GDPR), which enforces transparency and accountability in automated decision-making.
elements.sent-2411-046: Further advancements include interpretability methods like SHAP, LIME, and Grad-CAM, which aim to make complex models more accessible to stakeholders.
elements.sent-2411-048: Current interpretability tools often provide limited insights for complex models, display inconsistencies in high-dimensional data, and are not always applicable in real-time settings.
elements.sent-2411-049: Additionally, the rapid evolution of AI technologies outpaces regulatory responses, creating oversight gaps, especially with advanced models such as large language models (LLMs).
elements.sent-2411-050: These challenges underscore the ongoing need for innovations in responsible AI practices and supportive policy development.
elements.sent-2411-051: In their work, Madsen et al.~\cite{madsen2024interpretability advocate for moving beyond traditional interpretability methods, introducing three paradigms that focus on faithful model explanations: models with inherent faithfulness measures, those trained to provide faithful explanations, and self-explaining models.}
elements.sent-2411-052: Their work highlights that trustworthy AI requires explanations closely aligned with the model’s actual decision-making to prevent misunderstandings and misplaced trust.
elements.sent-2411-053: Whereas, Singh et al.~\cite{Singh2024RethinkingII explore interpretability challenges in large language models (LLMs), proposing tailored methods to address their scale and complexity, enabling clearer insights into model behavior.}
elements.sent-2411-054: Tull et al.~\cite{Tull2024TowardsCI present a category theory-based framework to unify interpretability approaches, aiming for a cohesive understanding of model behavior crucial to responsible AI.}
elements.sent-2411-055: Dinu et al.~\cite{Dinu2020ChallengingCI critically examine assumptions in feature attribution, finding that some methods lack reliability, thus stressing the need for rigorous evaluation of interpretability techniques.}
elements.sent-2411-056: While, Kaur et al.~\cite{Kaur2022SensibleAR apply sensemaking theory to AI, promoting explanations that align with human cognitive processes to enhance trust and accountability by making model reasoning more accessible.}
elements.sent-2411-057: AI safety aims to ensure AI systems are predictable, controllable, and aligned with human values, especially in critical areas like healthcare, autonomous vehicles, and infrastructure.
elements.sent-2411-058: A key aspect of AI safety is explainability, as transparent models enable developers, users, and regulators to understand system behavior, detect risks, and implement safeguards.
elements.sent-2411-059: Core pillars of AI safety include robustness, reliability, alignment with human intentions, and the use of explainability to clarify decision processes.
elements.sent-2411-060: Explainability is essential for risk mitigation in dynamic environments, where understanding AI behavior allows for safe intervention.
elements.sent-2411-061: For example, \cite{Amodei2016ConcretePI discusses challenges like reward hacking and exploration hazards in reinforcement learning.}
elements.sent-2411-062: Explainability techniques help analyze reward structures and behavior patterns, enabling developers to detect and correct unintended actions.
elements.sent-2411-063: Catastrophic forgetting, where models lose prior knowledge when learning new tasks, poses risks in sequential learning \cite{doi:10.1073/pnas.1611835114 .}
elements.sent-2411-064: Explainable AI (XAI) methods can identify model areas vulnerable to forgetting, supporting memory mechanisms that preserve safety-critical information.
elements.sent-2411-065: Explainability tools provide insights into reward structures’ impact on agent behavior, aiding iterative refinement to align model goals with human values.
elements.sent-2411-066: Explainability also supports adversarial robustness by revealing patterns in model vulnerability, guiding defenses that enhance safety and reliability.
elements.sent-2411-067: These examples underscore explainability’s role in AI safety, enhancing transparency, accountability, and risk mitigation.
elements.sent-2411-068: Embedding explainable practices within AI safety frameworks helps developers control AI behavior, reducing risks in diverse operational contexts.
elements.sent-2411-069: Explainable AI (XAI) is crucial for regulatory compliance, promoting transparency, fairness, and accountability in AI-driven decisions in sectors like finance, healthcare, and law.
elements.sent-2411-070: Regulatory frameworks increasingly mandate interpretable models to ensure oversight, protect user rights, and uphold ethical standards.
elements.sent-2411-071: Key elements of XAI in regulatory contexts include transparent decision processes, model auditability, and mechanisms to mitigate bias.
elements.sent-2411-072: In finance, XAI helps institutions clarify decisions on credit scoring, loan approvals, and fraud detection, reducing regulatory risks and fostering public trust.
elements.sent-2411-073: For instance, \cite{Weber2023ApplicationsOE systematically reviews XAI applications in finance, illustrating transparency's role in regulatory compliance.}
elements.sent-2411-074: As large language models (LLMs) become ubiquitous, interpretability in NLP has gained importance.
elements.sent-2411-075: Stakeholder-centered frameworks help ensure more responsible AI deployment.
elements.sent-2411-076: In healthcare, XAI is vital for patient safety and ethical standards.
elements.sent-2411-077: Explainable models enable healthcare providers to interpret AI-driven diagnoses, treatments, and risk assessments, aligning these with medical regulations.
elements.sent-2411-078: This study calls for standardized metrics to enhance explanation reliability, especially in high-stakes fields like healthcare and finance.
elements.sent-2411-079: Specific to LLMs, \cite{Singh2024RethinkingII explores their potential for interactive, natural language explanations that can improve comprehension of complex behaviors.}
elements.sent-2411-080: The authors address interpretability challenges, such as hallucinations and computational cost, recommending LLM-based methods to improve transparency and nuanced insights in high-accountability domains.
elements.sent-2411-081: Finally, \cite{Sixt2019WhenEL critiques common interpretability techniques, highlighting limitations in methods like Layer-wise Relevance Propagation (LRP) \cite{Montavon2019LayerWiseRP and proposing Cosine Similarity Convergence (CSC) as a metric to improve explanation accuracy.}}
elements.sent-2411-082: These studies emphasize XAI's role in enhancing regulatory compliance by fostering transparency, accountability, and fairness.
elements.sent-2411-083: Embedding explainable practices in regulatory frameworks ensures ethical AI use, facilitates oversight, and builds trust across regulated sectors.
elements.sent-2411-084: In high-stakes applications such as finance and healthcare, machine learning models like regression and probabilistic algorithms (e.g., decision trees and their variants) are often preferred.
elements.sent-2411-085: This is because deep learning models are frequently described as "black boxes," making it challenging to interpret how they arrive at their conclusions.
elements.sent-2411-086: This lack of transparency can be particularly problematic in critical contexts where accountability and trust are essential.
elements.sent-2411-087: To enhance interpretability, explainable algorithms like LIME~\cite{Ribeiro2016WhySI and SHAP~\cite{Lundberg2017AUA are increasingly used.}}
elements.sent-2411-088: LIME (Local Interpretable Model-Agnostic Explanations) builds simple, interpretable models around specific data points to highlight the most influential features for each prediction.
elements.sent-2411-089: SHAP (SHapley Additive exPlanations) assigns importance scores to each feature, providing both global explanations (by ranking features based on overall importance) and local explanations (by illustrating how individual features contribute to specific predictions).
elements.sent-2411-091: LIME’s reliance on random sampling can lead to inconsistent explanations for the same data point, and its effectiveness can be sensitive to the choice of perturbation method.
elements.sent-2411-092: SHAP, while comprehensive, can be computationally expensive for large datasets and complex models.
elements.sent-2411-093: Additionally, SHAP’s model-agnostic nature may result in less accurate explanations for highly intricate models, like deep neural networks.
elements.sent-2411-094: As a result, both LIME and SHAP may face challenges in providing precise, interpretable explanations for complex deep learning models.
elements.sent-2411-095: In explainable AI (XAI) for image modality-based tasks, gradient-based methods such as GradCAM~\cite{Selvaraju2016GradCAMVE , Vanilla Gradient~\cite{Simonyan2013DeepIC , SmoothGrad~\cite{Smilkov2017SmoothGradRN , and Integrated Gradients~\cite{Sundararajan2017AxiomaticAF are widely used for interpreting model predictions.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-096: GradCAM generates heatmaps by calculating the gradient of the target class with respect to convolutional layer activations, but may miss fine details and is sensitive to input noise.
elements.sent-2411-097: Vanilla Gradient directly computes gradients on the input, though it faces the "saturation problem," where gradients become too small for clear interpretation.
elements.sent-2411-098: SmoothGrad improves clarity by averaging gradients with added noise, albeit at a computational cost, while Integrated Gradients addresses saturation by calculating an integral of gradients from a baseline to the input, though it also demands significant computation.
elements.sent-2411-099: In recent advances, Vision Transformers (ViTs) require specific interpretability approaches due to their reliance on attention mechanisms.
elements.sent-2411-100: In the paper \cite{Wu2024TokenTM , authors introduces TokenTM, a method designed for ViTs that considers both token transformations (changes in token length and direction) and attention weights across layers.}
elements.sent-2411-101: By aggregating these factors, TokenTM provides more focused and reliable explanations, addressing unique interpretability challenges in transformer models.
elements.sent-2411-102: These developments reflect a shift in XAI, where interpretability techniques are tailored to the unique demands of model architectures, like CNNs and transformers, enhancing transparency and reliability across different models.
elements.sent-2411-103: In the text modality, quite a few explanation methods are employed to enhance the interpretability of machine learning models.
elements.sent-2411-104: Among these, LIME~\cite{Ribeiro2016WhySI and SHAP~\cite{Lundberg2017AUA are baseline for interpreting text classification models.}}
elements.sent-2411-105: Gradient-based methods, such as GradCAM~\cite{Selvaraju2016GradCAMVE , Integrated Gradients~\cite{Sundararajan2017AxiomaticAF , and Attention Rollout~\cite{Abnar2020QuantifyingAF , also play a significant role across diverse model architectures.}}}
elements.sent-2411-106: For text generation tasks, 17 challenges were identified by \cite{Amara2024ChallengesAO , such as tokenization effects and randomness, and advocates for probabilistic explanations and perturbed benchmarks to address these issues.}
elements.sent-2411-107: Furthermore, LACOAT introduced by \cite{Yu2024LatentCE , which clusters word representations to produce context-aware explanations.}
elements.sent-2411-108: It maps test features to latent clusters and translates these into natural language summaries, improving interpretability for complex NLP tasks.
elements.sent-2411-109: While \cite{Bogaert2024AQO draws attention to the noise in explanations generated by large language models (LLMs), noting significant randomness.}
elements.sent-2411-110: The study suggests that more sophisticated interpretability techniques, beyond simple word-level explanations, are needed to achieve reliability.
elements.sent-2411-111: Lastly, \cite{Sba2024HybridAT proposes a hybrid approach that combines counterfactual explanations with domain knowledge.}
elements.sent-2411-112: This method generates context-specific counterfactuals and incorporates user feedback, enhancing BERT’s interpretability through expert insights and interactive elements, thus fostering a more transparent framework.
elements.sent-2411-113: In addition to the previously discussed methods, Mechanistic Interpretability has become a cornerstone of research aimed at unraveling the internal mechanisms of large language models (LLMs).
elements.sent-2411-114: This field focuses on dissecting how specific components, such as neurons and attention heads, contribute to a model's functionality and decision-making processes.
elements.sent-2411-115: For example, Olah et al. \cite{olah2020zoom performed an in-depth analysis of individual neurons in LLMs, revealing how certain neurons specialize in detecting specific linguistic features.}
elements.sent-2411-116: Their work highlights the modularity and specialization inherent in these models.
elements.sent-2411-117: Building on this foundation, Elhage et al. \cite{Anthropic introduced the concept of "induction heads" in transformer architectures.}
elements.sent-2411-118: They demonstrated that these attention heads play a critical role in tasks such as sequence copying, offering insight into the mechanisms underlying certain model behaviors.
elements.sent-2411-119: Further advancing the field, Nanda et al. \cite{Nanda2023ProgressMF investigated the phenomenon of "grokking" in LLMs, where models suddenly exhibit strong generalization capabilities after extended training.}
elements.sent-2411-120: Their analysis traced the internal changes leading to this abrupt performance improvement, shedding light on this intriguing aspect of model behavior.
elements.sent-2411-121: These advancements underscore the significance of mechanistic interpretability in enhancing our understanding of the intricate operations of LLMs.
elements.sent-2411-122: By making AI systems more transparent and reliable, this research is vital for fostering trust, particularly as LLMs are scaled and deployed in high-stakes applications.
elements.sent-2411-123: Recent advancements in Explainable AI (XAI) emphasize the need for robust evaluation frameworks, benchmarks, and specialized toolkits to enhance transparency and trust in machine learning systems.
elements.sent-2411-124: Quantus \cite{Hedstrm2022QuantusAE , provides a modular toolkit with metrics such as faithfulness, robustness, and completeness, promoting reproducible and responsible evaluation of neural network explanations.}
elements.sent-2411-125: BEExAI \cite{Sithakoul2024BEExAIBT addresses the underexplored domain of tabular data by benchmarking explanation methods using tailored datasets and metrics like local fidelity and human interpretability, enabling systematic comparisons in real-world scenarios.}
elements.sent-2411-126: In a survey over 30 XAI toolkits by \cite{Le2023BenchmarkingEA , including Quantus, highlighting challenges such as inconsistent metrics, lack of fairness assessments, and insufficient focus on human-centered evaluation, calling for standardized benchmarks and unified platforms.}
elements.sent-2411-127: PyXAI \cite{audemard:hal-04680394 complements these efforts by focusing on tree-based models, introducing efficient tools and unique metrics such as path importance and rule-level interpretability for domains like healthcare and finance.}
elements.sent-2411-128: Despite these advancements, key gaps remain, including standardization across tools, evaluation for diverse data types, human-centered usability, and fairness and robustness assessments, underscoring the need for continued research to achieve actionable and responsible XAI.
elements.figure-2411-001: \begin{subfigure}{8cm} \includegraphics[width=7.35cm]{eg2.png} \caption{Sample Network} \label{fig:image1} \end{subfigure} \begin{subfigure}{8cm} \includegraphics[width=7.35cm]{eg1.png} \caption{Relevance Ouptput for Sample Network} \label{fig:image2} \end{subfigure} \caption{Illustration Depecting Backtrace Calculation for a Sample Network} \label{fig:two_images}
elements.sent-2411-129: Backtrace is a technique for analyzing neural networks that involves tracing the relevance of each component from the output back to the input.
elements.sent-2411-130: This approach clarifies how each element contributes to the final prediction.
elements.sent-2411-131: By distributing relevance scores across various layers, Backtrace provides insights into feature importance, information flow, and potential biases, which facilitates improved model interpretation and validation without relying on external dependencies.
elements.sent-2411-132: Backtrace has the following advantages over other available tools:.
elements.sent-2411-133: No dependence on a sample selection algorithm : The relevance is calculated using just the sample in focus.
elements.sent-2411-134: This avoids deviations in importance due to varying trends in sample datasets.
elements.sent-2411-135: No dependence on a secondary white-box algorithm : The relevance is calculated directly from the network itself.
elements.sent-2411-136: This prevents any variation in importance due to type, hyperparameters and assumptions of secondary algorithms.
elements.sent-2411-137: Deterministic in nature The relevance scores won’t change on repeated calculations on the same sample.
elements.sent-2411-138: Hence, can be used in live environments or training workflows as a result of its independence from external factors.
elements.sent-2411-139: Backtrace operates in two modes: Default Mode and Contrast Mode.
elements.sent-2411-140: First we describe the Basic Methodology of Backtrace in Default Mode as follows :.
elements.sent-2411-141: Every neural network consists of multiple layers.
elements.sent-2411-142: Each layer has a variation of the following basic operation: \begin{equation}y = \Phi(Wx + b)\end{equation} where,.
elements.sent-2411-143: This can be further organized as: \begin{equation}y = \Phi(X_p + X_n + b)\end{equation} where,.
elements.sent-2411-144: Activation functions can be categorized into monotonic and non-monotonic functions.
elements.sent-2411-145: Non-Monotonic functions: The relevance is propagated as is.
elements.sent-2411-146: Monotonic functions: The relevance is switched off for positive or negative components based on saturation.
elements.sent-2411-147: The aforementioned modes represent the basic operations at each source layer for propagating relevance to the destination layer.
elements.sent-2411-148: The procedure for relevance calculation is as follows:.
elements.sent-2411-149: Construct a graph from the model weights and architecture with output nodes as root and input nodes as leaves.
elements.sent-2411-150: Propagate relevance in a breadth-first manner, starting at the root.
elements.sent-2411-151: The propagation completes when all leaves (input nodes) have been assigned relevance.
elements.sent-2411-152: Note: Any loss of relevance during propagation is due to network bias.
elements.sent-2411-153: The relevance of a single sample represents local importance.
elements.sent-2411-154: For global importance, the relevance of each feature can be aggregated after normalization at the sample level.
elements.sent-2411-155: The algorithm has two modes of operation:.
elements.sent-2411-156: In this mode, a single relevance is associated with each unit.
elements.sent-2411-157: The relevance is propagated by proportionately distributing it between positive and negative components.
elements.sent-2411-158: If the relevance associated with $y$ is $r_y$ and with $x$ is $r_x$, then for the $j$th unit in $y$, we compute: \begin{equation}T_j = X_{pj} + |X_{nj}| + |b_j|\end{equation} \begin{equation}R_{pj} = \frac{X_{pj}}{T_j} r_{yj}, \quad R_{nj} = \frac{X_{nj}}{T_j} r_{yj}, \quad R_{bj} = \frac{b_j}{T_j} r_{yj}\end{equation} $R_{pj}$ and $R_{nj}$ are distributed among $x$ in the following manner: \begin{equation}r_{xij} = \begin{cases} \frac{W_{ij} x_{ij}}{X_{pj}} R_{pj} \text{if } W_{ij} x_{ij} > 0 0 \text{if } W_{ij} x_{ij} > 0 \text{ and } \Phi \text{ is saturated on negative end} \frac{-W_{ij} x_{ij}}{X_{nj}} R_{nj} \text{if } W_{ij} x_{ij} < 0 0 \text{if } W_{ij} x_{ij} < 0 \text{ and } \Phi \text{ is saturated on positive end} 0 \text{if } W_{ij} x_{ij} = 0 \end{cases}\end{equation} The total relevance at layer $x$ is: \begin{equation}r_{x} = \sum_i r_{xi}\end{equation}.
elements.formula-2411-005: \begin{equation}r_{xij} =  \frac{W_{ij} x_{ij}}{X_{pj}} R_{pj} if  W_{ij} x_{ij} > 0 0 if  W_{ij} x_{ij} > 0  and  \Phi  is saturated on negative end \frac{-W_{ij} x_{ij}}{X_{nj}} R_{nj} if  W_{ij} x_{ij} < 0 0 if  W_{ij} x_{ij} < 0  and  \Phi  is saturated on positive end 0 if  W_{ij} x_{ij} = 0 \end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-159: In this mode, each unit is assigned dual relevance, distributed between positive and negative components.
elements.sent-2411-160: This approach facilitates separate analyses of supporting and detracting influences.
elements.sent-2411-161: Unlike single-mode propagation, which combines relevance into aggregated scores, dual-mode propagation provides clarity by isolating favorable and adverse contributions.
elements.sent-2411-162: This separation enhances interpretability, enabling deeper insights into features that negatively impact predictions a capability essential for identifying counterfactuals or assessing model biases in high-stakes scenarios.
elements.sent-2411-163: If the relevance associated with $y$ are $r_{yp , r_{yn}$ and with $x$ are $r_{xp , r_{xn}$, then for the $j$th unit in $y$, we compute: \begin{equation}T_j = X_{pj} + X_{nj} + b_j\end{equation} We then calculate Determine $R_{pj}$, $R_{nj}$, and Relevance Polarity as described in Algorithm~\ref{alg:cont1 .}}}
elements.algorithm-2411-001: [H] \caption{Determine $R_{pj}$, $R_{nj}$, and relevance polarity in Contrastive Mode} \begin{algorithmic}[1] \If{$T_j > 0$} \If{$r_{ypj} > r_{ynj}$} \State $R_{pj} \gets r_{ypj}$ \State $R_{nj} \gets r_{ynj}$ \State $\text{relevance\_polarity} \gets 1$ \Else \State $R_{pj} \gets r_{ynj}$ \State $R_{nj} \gets r_{ypj}$ \State $\text{relevance\_polarity} \gets -1$ \EndIf \Else \If{$r_{ypj} > r_{ynj}$} \State $R_{pj} \gets r_{ynj}$ \State $R_{nj} \gets r_{ypj}$ \State $\text{relevance\_polarity} \gets -1$ \Else \State $R_{pj} \gets r_{ypj}$ \State $R_{nj} \gets r_{ynj}$ \State $\text{relevance\_polarity} \gets 1$ \EndIf \EndIf \end{algorithmic} \label{alg:cont1}
elements.sent-2411-164: Afterwards, $R_{pj}$ and $R_{nj}$ are distributed among $x$ as described in Algorithm~\ref{alg:cont2 .}
elements.algorithm-2411-002: [H] \caption{Computation of $r_{xp,ij}$ and $r_{xn,ij}$ based on relevance polarity} \begin{algorithmic}[1] \If{$\text{relevance\_polarity} > 0$} \State $r_{xp,ij} \gets \frac{W_{ij} x_{ij}}{X_{pj}} R_{pj} \quad \forall W_{ij} x_{ij} > 0$ \State $r_{xn,ij} \gets \frac{-W_{ij} x_{ij}}{X_{nj}} R_{nj} \quad \forall W_{ij} x_{ij} < 0$ \Else \State $r_{xp,ij} \gets \frac{-W_{ij} x_{ij}}{X_{nj}} R_{nj} \quad \forall W_{ij} x_{ij} < 0$ \State $r_{xn,ij} \gets \frac{W_{ij} x_{ij}}{X_{pj}} R_{pj} \quad \forall W_{ij} x_{ij} > 0$ \EndIf \end{algorithmic} \label{alg:cont2}
elements.sent-2411-165: The total positive and negative relevance at layer $x$ are: \begin{equation}r_{xp} = \sum_i r_{xp,i}, \quad r_{xn} = \sum_i r_{xn,i}\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-166: Currently, the majority of AI models across various applications are primarily based on the attention mechanism \cite{Vaswani2017AttentionIA .}
elements.sent-2411-167: Accordingly, we have extended our Backtrace algorithm to provide support for this attention model~\cite{Achtibat2024AttnLRPAL .}
elements.sent-2411-168: Attention mechanism allows the model to focus on specific parts of the input sequence, dynamically weighting the importance of different elements when making predictions.
elements.sent-2411-169: The attention function employs the equation: \begin{equation}Attention(Q, K, V) = \text{softmax}\left(\frac{QK^T}{\sqrt{d_k}}\right)V\end{equation} For Multi-Head Attention, the implementation is as follows: \begin{equation}MultiHead(Q, K, V) = Concat\left(head_1, head_2,..., head_n\right){W^O}\end{equation} where each head is computed as \begin{equation}head_i = \text{Attention}\left(QW_i^Q, KW_i^K, VW_i^V\right)\end{equation} such that.
elements.sent-2411-170: Q, K, V: Query, Key, Value Matrices.
elements.sent-2411-171: Suppose the input to the attention layer is \( x \) and the output is \( y \).
elements.sent-2411-172: The relevance associated with \( y \) is \( r_y \).
elements.sent-2411-173: To compute the relevance using the Backtrace, we use the steps as indicated in Algorithm~\ref{alg:cont3 below:.}
elements.algorithm-2411-003: [H] \caption{Relevance Propagation for Attention Layers} \begin{algorithmic}[1] \State Input: \( x \) (input to attention layer) \State Output: \( r_y \) (relevance associated with \( y \)) \State We calculate the relevance \( r_O \) of \( Concat\left(head_1, head_2, \ldots, head_n\right) \).Where \( r_O \) represents the relevance from the linear projection layer of the Attention module. \State To compute the relevance of \( QK^T \) and \( V \), use the following formulas: \begin{equation} r_{QK} = \left(r_O \cdot x_V\right) \cdot x_{QK} \end{equation} \begin{equation} r_V = \left(x_{QK} \cdot r_O\right) \cdot x_{V} \end{equation} \textit{Here, \( x_{QK} \) and \( x_V \) are the outputs of \( QK^T \) and \( V \), respectively.} \State Now that we have \( r_{QK} \), compute the relevance of \( r_Q \) and \( r_K \) as: \begin{equation} r_Q = \left(r_{QK} \cdot x_Q\right) \cdot x_K \end{equation} \begin{equation} r_K = \left(x_K \cdot r_{QK}\right) \cdot x_Q \end{equation} Here, \( x_Q \) and \( x_K \) are the outputs of \( Q \) and \( K \), respectively. \State To compute \( r_{Attn} \), sum up \( r_Q \), \( r_K \), and \( r_V \): \begin{equation} r_{Attn} = r_Q + r_K + r_V \end{equation} \end{algorithmic} \label{alg:cont3}
elements.sent-2411-174: In this section, we present a comparative study to benchmark our proposed Backtrace algorithm against various existing explainability methods.
elements.sent-2411-175: The goal of this evaluation is to assess the effectiveness, robustness, and interpretability of Backtrace in providing meaningful insights into model predictions across different data modalities, including tabular, image, and text data.
elements.sent-2411-176: By systematically comparing our approach with established methods, we aim to highlight the advantages and potential limitations of Backtrace in the context of explainable artificial intelligence (XAI).
elements.sent-2411-177: The experimental setup consists of three distinct data modalities: tabular, image, and text.
elements.sent-2411-178: Each modality is associated with specific tasks, datasets, and model architectures tailored to effectively evaluate the explainability methods.
elements.sent-2411-179: For the tabular data modality, we focus on a binary classification task utilizing the Lending Club dataset.
elements.sent-2411-180: This dataset is representative of financial applications, containing features that capture various attributes of borrower profiles.
elements.sent-2411-181: We employ a four-layer Multi-Layer Perceptron (MLP) neural network, which is well-suited for learning from structured data and provides a foundation for assessing the performance of explainability techniques.
elements.sent-2411-182: In the image data modality, we conduct a multi-class classification task using the CIFAR-10 dataset.
elements.sent-2411-183: This benchmark dataset consists of images across 10 different classes, making it ideal for evaluating image classification algorithms.
elements.sent-2411-184: For this experiment, we utilize a fine-tuned ResNet-34 model, known for its deep residual learning capabilities, which enhances the model's ability to learn intricate patterns and features within the images.
elements.sent-2411-185: The text data modality involves a binary classification task using the SST-2 dataset, which is focused on sentiment analysis.
elements.sent-2411-186: The dataset consists of movie reviews labeled as positive or negative, allowing for a nuanced evaluation of sentiment classification models.
elements.sent-2411-187: We employ a pre-trained BERT model, which leverages transformer-based architectures to capture contextual relationships in text.
elements.sent-2411-188: This approach facilitates the generation of high-quality explanations for the model's predictions, enabling a thorough assessment of explainability methods in the realm of natural language processing.
elements.sent-2411-189: To assess the effectiveness of explanation methods across various modalities, we utilize different metrics tailored to specific use cases.
elements.sent-2411-191: Maximal Perturbation Robustness Test (MPRT): This metric assesses the extent of perturbation that can be applied to an input before there are significant changes in the model's explanation of its decision.
elements.sent-2411-192: It evaluates the stability and robustness of the model's explanations rather than solely its predictions.
elements.sent-2411-193: Complexity Metric: This metric quantifies the level of detail in a model's explanation by analyzing the distribution of feature contributions.
elements.sent-2411-194: Faithfulness Correlation: Faithfulness Correlation \cite{Bhatt2020EvaluatingAA metric evaluates the degree to which an explanation aligns with the model’s behavior by calculating the correlation between feature importance and changes in model output resulting from perturbations of key features.}
elements.sent-2411-195: Max Sensitivity: Max-Sensitivity \cite{Yeh2019OnT , is a robustness metric for explainability methods that evaluates how sensitive explanations are to small perturbations in the input.}
elements.sent-2411-196: Using a Monte Carlo sampling-based approximation, it measures the maximum change in the explanation when slight random modifications are applied to the input.
elements.sent-2411-197: Formally, it computes the maximum distance (e.g., using \(L_1\), \(L_2\), or \(L_\infty\) norms) between the original explanation and those derived from perturbed inputs.
elements.sent-2411-198: Pixel Flipping: Pixel Flipping method involves perturbing significant pixels and measuring the degradation in the model’s prediction, thereby testing the robustness of the generated explanation.
elements.sent-2411-199: To evaluate the textual modality, we use the Token Perturbation for Explanation Quality (ToPEQ) metric, which assesses the robustness of model explanations by analyzing the impact of token perturbations.
elements.sent-2411-200: We employ the Least Relevant First AUC (LeRF AUC) and Most Relevant First AUC (MoRF AUC) to measure sensitivity to the least and most important tokens, respectively.
elements.sent-2411-201: Additionally, we calculate Delta AUC, the difference between LeRF AUC and MoRF AUC, to further indicate the model's ability to distinguish between important and unimportant features.
elements.sent-2411-202: LeRF AUC (Least Relevant First AUC): This metric evaluates how gradually perturbing the least important features (tokens) affects the model's confidence.
elements.sent-2411-203: The AUC measures the model’s response as the least relevant features are replaced with a baseline (e.g., [UNK]), indicating the degree to which the model relies on these features.
elements.sent-2411-204: MoRF AUC (Most Relevant First AUC): This metric measures how quickly the model's performance deteriorates when the most important features are perturbed first.
elements.sent-2411-205: The AUC represents the decrease in the model's confidence as the most relevant tokens are removed, revealing the impact of these key features on the prediction.
elements.sent-2411-206: Delta AUC: This metric represents the difference between LeRF AUC and MoRF AUC.
elements.sent-2411-207: It reflects the model's sensitivity to the removal of important features (MoRF) compared to less important ones (LeRF).
elements.sent-2411-208: A larger delta suggests that the explanation method effectively distinguishes between important and unimportant features.
elements.sent-2411-209: We evaluated 1,024 samples from the test set of the Lending Club dataset, using a fine-tuned MLP checkpoint that attained an accuracy of 0.89 and a weighted average F1 score of 0.87.
elements.sent-2411-210: We assessed Backtrace against widely used metrics for tabular data, specifically LIME and SHAP \cite{Lundberg2017AUA , and employed MPRT for comparison, along with Complexity to examine the simplicity of the model explanations as illustrated in Appendix~\ref{sec:tab-samples .}}

elements.sent-2411-212: This highlights its improved interpretability and robustness in explainability.
elements.sent-2411-213: However, Backtrace exhibits higher computational complexity, reflecting the fine-grained, higher entropy of its explanations.
elements.sent-2411-214: This trade-off suggests the need to balance the quality of interpretability with the simplicity of model explanations.
elements.sent-2411-215: We conducted an evaluation on 500 samples from the CIFAR-10 test set using a supervised, fine-tuned ResNet-34 model, which achieved a test accuracy of 75.85\%.

elements.sent-2411-219: Backtrace achieves a superior Faithfulness Correlation score of (0.199), indicating a stronger alignment between its explanations and the model’s behavior.
elements.sent-2411-220: Additionally, it demonstrates robust performance with much lower Max Sensitivity (0.617) and Pixel Flipping (0.199) scores, highlighting its stability against input perturbations and better robustness in preserving the model’s predictive integrity under pixel modifications.
elements.sent-2411-221: Overall, Backtrace establishes itself as a more reliable and robust explainability technique for image modality tasks.
elements.sent-2411-222: For the text modality, evaluation was performed on the evaluation set of the SST-2 dataset using a fine-tuned BERT model\footnote{Model checkpoint used \url{https://huggingface.co/textattack/bert-base-uncased-SST-2 , achieving an F1 score of 0.926.}}
elements.sent-2411-223: Since explainable AI (XAI) for BERT and other transformer-based models is relatively new, we employed metrics based on token perturbation for explanation quality, specifically LeRF, MoRF, and Delta AUC, as introduced in \cite{Achtibat2024AttnLRPAL .}
elements.sent-2411-224: We used methods like IG, SmoothGrad, AttnRoll, GradCAM and Input Grad as illustrated in Appendix~\ref{sec:text-samples .}

elements.sent-2411-225: As shown in Table~\ref{tab:performance_metrics_text , Integrated Gradients (IG) delivered the strongest performance, achieving the lowest MoRF AUC and the highest LeRF and Delta AUC, underscoring the robustness of its explanations and precise feature attribution.}
elements.sent-2411-226: Smooth Grad and Attn Roll also demonstrated commendable performance.
elements.sent-2411-227: Backtrace exhibited balanced results across LeRF and Delta AUC metrics, with a MoRF AUC of 15.431, showcasing its ability to provide meaningful explanations while allowing scope for further enhancement.
elements.sent-2411-228: These findings highlight the effectiveness of IG in explainability for transformer-based models while recognizing Backtrace's potential as a promising and competitive approach.
elements.sent-2411-229: The quality of explanations is influenced by both the input data and the model weights.
elements.sent-2411-230: The impact of model performance is significant; low model performance tends to result in unstable explanations characterized by high entropy, while good model performance is associated with stable explanations that are more sparse.
elements.sent-2411-231: Additionally, the inference time for a sample is proportional to both the size of the model and the computational infrastructure used.
elements.sent-2411-232: Additional illustrations of Backtrace for various use cases are provided in Appendix~\ref{sec:illustrations .}
elements.sent-2411-233: Existing solutions involve distribution graphs and heatmaps for any network node based on node activation.
elements.sent-2411-234: These are accurate for that specific node but don't represent the impact of that node on the final prediction.
elements.sent-2411-235: Existing solutions are also unable to differentiate between the impact of input sources versus the internal network biases.
elements.sent-2411-236: With each input source being assigned a fraction of the overall weightage, we can now quantify the dependence of the final prediction on each input source.
elements.sent-2411-237: We can also evaluate the dependence within the input source as the weight assignment happens on a per unit basis.
elements.sent-2411-238: Integrated Gradients and Shapley values are other methods available for calculating feature importance from Deep Learning Models.
elements.sent-2411-239: Both come with caveats and give approximate values: \begin{itemize.}
elements.sent-2411-240: Integrated Gradients depends on a baseline sample which needs to be constructed for the dataset and altered as the dataset shifts.
elements.sent-2411-241: This is extremely difficult for high-dimensional datasets.
elements.sent-2411-242: Shapley Values are calculated on a sample set selected from the complete dataset.
elements.sent-2411-243: This makes those values highly dependent on the selection of data.
elements.sent-2411-244: Instead of just relying on the final prediction score for decision-making, the validity of the decision can now be determined based on the weight distribution of any particular node with respect to the prior distribution of correct and incorrect predictions.
elements.sent-2411-245: The Backtrace framework is applicable in the following use-cases:.
elements.subsubsection-2411-019: Interpreting the model outcomes using the local and global importance of each feature
elements.sent-2411-246: The local importance is directly inferred from the relevance associated with input data layers.
elements.sent-2411-247: For inferring global importance, the local importance of each sample is normalized with respect to the model outcome of that sample.
elements.sent-2411-248: The normalized local importance from all samples is then averaged to provide global importance.
elements.sent-2411-249: The averaging can be further graded based on the various outcomes and binning of the model outcome.
elements.subsubsection-2411-020: Network analysis based on the relevance attributed to each layer in the network
elements.sent-2411-250: The two modes together provide a lot of information for each layer, such as:.
elements.sent-2411-252: Positive and negative relevance (unit-wise and complete layer).
elements.sent-2411-253: Using this information, layers can be modified to increase or decrease variability and reduce network bias.
elements.sent-2411-254: Major changes to the network architecture via complete shutdown of nodes or pathways are also possible based on the total contribution of that component.
elements.subsubsection-2411-021: Fairness and bias analysis using the feature-wise importance
elements.sent-2411-255: This is in continuation of the global importance of features.
elements.sent-2411-256: Based on the global importance of sensitive features (e.g. gender, age, etc.) and their alignment with the data, it can be inferred whether the model or data have undue bias towards any feature value.
elements.subsubsection-2411-022: Process Compliance based on the ranking of features on local and global levels
elements.sent-2411-257: Using the local and global importance of features and ranking them accordingly, it can be determined whether the model is considering the features in the same manner as in the business process it is emulating.
elements.sent-2411-258: This also helps in evaluating the solution’s alignment with various business and regulatory requirements.
elements.sent-2411-259: Every model is analyzed based on certain performance metrics which are calculated over a compiled validation dataset.
elements.sent-2411-260: This doesn’t represent the live deployment scenario.
elements.sent-2411-261: During deployment, validation of outcomes is extremely important for complete autonomous systems.
elements.sent-2411-262: The layer-wise relevance can be used for accomplishing this.
elements.sent-2411-263: The relevance for each layer is mapped in the vector space of the same dimension as the layer outcome, yet it is linearly related to the model outcome.
elements.sent-2411-264: Since the information changes as it passes through the network, the relevance from lower layers, even input layers, can be used to get different outcomes.
elements.sent-2411-265: These outcomes can be used to validate the model outcome.
elements.sent-2411-266: The layers are generally multi-dimensional, for which either proximity-based methods or white-box regression algorithms can be used to derive outcomes.
elements.sent-2411-267: In this paper, we introduced the DLBacktrace, a new method that significantly improves model interpretability for deep learning.
elements.sent-2411-268: DLBacktrace traces relevance from output back to input, giving clear and consistent insights into which features are important and how information flows through the model.
elements.sent-2411-269: Unlike existing methods, which often rely on changing inputs or using other algorithms, DLBacktrace is stable and reliable, making it especially useful in fields that need high transparency, like finance, healthcare, and regulatory compliance.
elements.sent-2411-270: Our benchmarking results demonstrate that DLBacktrace performs better in terms of robustness and accuracy across various model types, proving it can provide practical insights.
elements.sent-2411-271: Overall, DLBacktrace contributes to the growing field of explainable AI by enhancing model transparency and trustworthiness, promoting responsible AI deployment in critical applications.
elements.sent-2411-272: Future research on DLBacktrace will aim to broaden its use and improve how it scores relevance.
elements.sent-2411-273: Key areas for development include adapting DLBacktrace for complex and evolving model architectures, like advanced transformers and multimodal models to ensure it remains effective across different AI applications.
elements.sent-2411-274: Additionally, we aim to reduce the inference time making DLBacktrace more suitable for real-time applications in production environments such as autonomous systems and dynamic decision-making scenarios.
elements.sent-2411-275: Future development will also explore the use of DLBacktrace for specific model improvements, including diagnosis and targeted editing.
elements.sent-2411-276: For example, DLBacktrace could assist in model pruning by identifying less critical components, tehreby optimizing model performance and efficiency.
elements.sent-2411-277: In addition, DLBacktrace's potential for targeted model improvements will be explored.
elements.sent-2411-278: It can assist in model pruning, especially for Mixtures of Experts (MoEs), by identifying underutilized components or redundant experts to optimize performance and efficiency.
elements.sent-2411-279: It can also help in facilitate model merging, providing insights for seamless integration of multiple models, and layer swapping, enabling selective replacement of layers to enhance adaptability or performance.
elements.sent-2411-280: We also plan to apply DLBacktrace to out-of-distribution (OOD) detection, where it can help distinguish instances that fall outside the model's training data, enhancing the robustness and reliability of AI systems.
elements.sent-2411-281: Furthermore, extending DLBacktrace's support for model-agnostic explainability will allow it to be seamlessly applied across various architectures, making it a versatile tool in explainable AI.
elements.sent-2411-282: These improvements will make DLBacktrace more useful and establish it as an important tool for understanding and improving models across a wide range of AI applications and tasks.. .

elements.sent-2411-283: The rapid growth of AI has led to more complex deep learning models, often operating as opaque "black boxes" with limited transparency in their decision-making.
elements.sent-2411-284: This lack of interpretability poses challenges, especially in high-stakes applications where understanding model output is crucial.
elements.sent-2411-285: This work highlights the importance of interpretability in fostering trust, accountability, and responsible deployment.
elements.sent-2411-286: To address these challenges, we introduce DLBacktrace, a novel, model-agnostic technique designed to provide clear insights into deep learning model decisions across a wide range of domains and architectures, including MLPs, CNNs, and Transformer-based LLM models.
elements.sent-2411-287: We present a comprehensive overview of DLBacktrace and benchmark its performance against established interpretability methods such as SHAP, LIME, and GradCAM.
elements.sent-2411-288: Our results demonstrate that DLBacktrace effectively enhances understanding of model behavior across diverse tasks.

elements.sent-2411-345: DLBacktrace is a technique for analyzing neural networks that involves tracing the relevance of each component from the output back to the input revealing the feature importance of input, the importance of each layer, information flow, and biases.
elements.sent-2411-346: It operates independently of external algorithms or datasets, ensuring consistency and deterministic results.
elements.sent-2411-347: Relevance is computed directly from the network, making DLBacktrace ideal for live environments and training workflows, with improved interpretation and validation.
elements.sent-2411-348: Utilizing DLBacktrace enables the extraction of local relevance for each sample, highlighting the importance unique to that specific instance.
elements.sent-2411-349: By aggregating the normalized scores across multiple samples, it effectively captures the global relevance.
elements.sent-2411-350: The procedure for relevance calculation for a given input sample and model (with trained weights) as illustrated in Figure ~\ref{fig:two_images is as follows:.}
elements.sent-2411-351: DLBacktrace works in two modes: Default and Contrastive.
elements.sent-2411-352: In Default Mode, a single relevance score is distributed proportionally across positive and negative components, influenced by saturation effects in activation functions.
elements.sent-2411-353: Contrastive Mode assigns dual relevance scores (positive and negative) to each unit, separating supportive and detractive contributions.
elements.sent-2411-354: This mode facilitates deeper analysis of negatively impactful features and enables bias detection or counterfactual evaluation in applications like sentiment analysis.
elements.sent-2411-355: Context: Every deep learning model consists of multiple layers.
elements.sent-2411-356: Each layer has a variation of the basic operation: \begin{equation}y = \Phi(Wx + b)\end{equation} where, $\Phi$ = activation function , $W$ = weight matrix of the layer , $b$ = bias , $x$ = input , $y$ = output .
elements.sent-2411-357: This can be further organized as: \begin{equation}y = \Phi(X_p + X_n + b)\end{equation} where, $X_p = \sum W_i x_i \quad \forall W_i x_i > 0$ and $X_n = \sum W_i x_i \quad \forall W_i x_i < 0$.
elements.sent-2411-358: If the relevance associated with $y$ are $r_{yp}$ $\And$ $r_{yn}$ and with $x$ are $r_{xp}$ $\And$ $r_{xn}$, then for the $j$th unit in $y$, we compute: \begin{equation}T_j = X_{pj} + X_{nj} + b_j\end{equation} \begin{equation}R_{pj} = \frac{X_{pj}}{T_j} r_{yj}, \quad R_{nj} = \frac{X_{nj}}{T_j} r_{yj}, \quad R_{bj} = \frac{b_j}{T_j} r_{yj}\end{equation} $R_{pj}$ and $R_{nj}$ are distributed among $x$ as follows: \begin{equation} r_{xij} = \begin{cases} \frac{W_{ij} x_{ij}}{X_{pj}} R_{pj} \text{if } W_{ij} x_{ij} > 0 0 \text{if } W_{ij} x_{ij} > 0 \text{ and } \Phi \text{ is saturated on negative end} \frac{-W_{ij} x_{ij}}{X_{nj}} R_{nj} \text{if } W_{ij} x_{ij} < 0 0 \text{if } W_{ij} x_{ij} < 0 \text{ and } \Phi \text{ is saturated on positive end} 0 \text{if } W_{ij} x_{ij} = 0 \end{cases}\end{equation} The total relevance at layer $x$ is: \begin{equation}r_{x} = \sum_i r_{xi}\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-359: If the relevance associated with $y$ are $r_{yp}$ $\And$ $r_{yn}$ and with $x$ are $r_{xp}$ $\And$ $r_{xn}$, then for the $j$th unit in $y$, we compute: \begin{equation}T_j = X_{pj} + X_{nj} + b_j\end{equation} We then determine $R_{pj}$, $R_{nj}$, and Relevance Polarity as described in Algorithm~\ref{alg:cont1 .}
elements.sent-2411-360: Most modern AI models rely on the attention mechanism \cite{Vaswani2017AttentionIA , which has been extended in our work to support the DLBacktrace algorithm.The attention mechanism allows the model to focus on relevant parts of the input sequence by dynamically adjusting the importance of elements for predictions.}
elements.sent-2411-361: The attention function is defined as: \begin{equation}Attention(Q, K, V) = \text{softmax}\left(\frac{QK^T}{\sqrt{d_k}}\right)V\end{equation} For Multi-Head Attention, the function is: \begin{equation}MultiHead(Q, K, V) = Concat\left(head_1, \ldots, head_n\right)W^O\end{equation} \begin{equation}head_i = \text{Attention}\left(QW_i^Q, KW_i^K, VW_i^V\right)\end{equation} Where, \( Q, K, V \): Query, Key, and Value matrices, \( W_i^Q, W_i^K, W_i^V \): Weight matrices for each head,\( W^O \): Weight matrix for combining all heads after concatenation and \text{Concat : Concatenates the outputs from all heads .}

elements.lstlisting-2411-004: layer_outputs = backtrace.predict(test_data[0]) relevance = backtrace.eval(layer_outputs, mode='default', scaler=1, thresholding=0.5, task="binary-classification")
elements.sent-2411-369: The relevance evaluation process in DLBacktrace involves adjusting four key attributes: mode, scaler, thresholding, model-type, and task.
elements.sent-2411-370: The mode attribute defines the method for evaluating relevance (e.g., "default," "contrastive"), determining how layer contributions are measured.
elements.sent-2411-371: The model-type is used for specific model types that require special operations during graph creation (e.g., 'BERT').
elements.sent-2411-372: The scaler attribute controls the scaling of relevance scores; it provides initial relevance value at the last layer (e.g., scaler=1).
elements.sent-2411-373: The thresholding attribute sets a cutoff for model confidence values, discarding predictions to select a predicted class below a specified threshold (e.g., thresholding=0.5 it is mostly used for segmentation task).
elements.sent-2411-374: The task attribute specifies the type of task being performed (e.g., "binary-classification", "regression", "binary-segmentation"), influencing how relevance is computed based on the model task.
elements.sent-2411-375: In this section, we present a comparative study to benchmark our proposed DLBacktrace algorithm against various existing explainability methods.
elements.sent-2411-376: The goal of this evaluation is to assess the effectiveness, robustness, and interpretability of DLBacktrace in providing meaningful insights into model predictions across different data modalities, including tabular, image, and text data.
elements.sent-2411-377: By systematically comparing our approach with established methods, we aim to highlight the advantages and potential limitations of DLBacktrace in the context of explainable artificial intelligence (XAI).

elements.sent-2411-383: We compared DLBacktrace against several methods as illustrated in Fig.~\ref{fig: Multi-class Classification Interpretations-2 , including Grad-CAM, vanilla gradient, smooth gradient, and integrated gradient.}
elements.sent-2411-384: The Evaluations conducted as shown in Table~\ref{tab:performance_metrics_image reveals that DLBacktrace significantly outperforms traditional methods like Grad-CAM, Vanilla Gradient, Smooth Gradient, and Integrated Gradient across all key metrics.}
elements.sent-2411-385: DLBacktrace achieves a superior Faithfulness Correlation score of (0.199), indicating a stronger alignment between its explanations and the model’s behavior.
elements.sent-2411-386: Overall, DLBacktrace establishes itself as a more reliable and robust explainability technique for image modality tasks.
elements.sent-2411-387: For the text modality, evaluation was performed on the evaluation set of the SST-2 dataset using a fine-tuned BERT model, achieving an F1 score of 0.926.
elements.sent-2411-388: We used methods like IG, SmoothGrad, AttnRoll, GradCAM and Input Grad as illustrated in Fig.~\ref{fig:bert_text .}
elements.sent-2411-389: DLBacktrace exhibited balanced results across LeRF and Delta AUC metrics, with a MoRF AUC of 15.431, showcasing its ability to provide meaningful explanations while allowing scope for further enhancement.
elements.sent-2411-390: These findings highlight the effectiveness of IG in explainability for transformer-based models while recognizing DLBacktrace's potential as a promising and competitive approach.
elements.sent-2411-391: DLBacktrace framework offers key advantages in model interpretation.
elements.sent-2411-392: It improves network analysis by addressing the limitations of traditional methods like distribution graphs and heatmaps, which only show node activations without reflecting their impact on the final prediction.
elements.sent-2411-393: It also distinguishes between input data and internal network biases.
elements.sent-2411-394: For feature importance, DLBacktrace assigns weights to input sources, making it easier to understand their influence on predictions, and avoids the issues seen with methods like Integrated Gradients and Shapley values.
elements.sent-2411-395: Additionally, it incorporates uncertainty by evaluating node weight distributions relative to past predictions.
elements.sent-2411-396: DLBacktrace is versatile and applicable in several areas.
elements.sent-2411-397: It provides local and global feature importance, with global importance averaged across samples.
elements.sent-2411-398: In network analysis, it offers insights into layer relevance, including bias-to-input ratios and activation saturation, guiding network adjustments.
elements.sent-2411-399: The framework also supports fairness and bias analysis by evaluating the influence of sensitive features like gender or age.
elements.sent-2411-400: It helps assess process compliance by comparing feature rankings with business rules or regulations.
elements.sent-2411-401: DLBacktrace has the following advantages over other available tools:.
elements.sent-2411-402: This prevents any variation in importance due to type, hyperparameters, and assumptions of secondary algorithms.
elements.sent-2411-403: Hence, it can be used in live environments or training workflows as a result of its independence from external factors.
elements.sent-2411-404: We can also evaluate the dependence within the input source as the weight assignment happens on a per-unit basis.
elements.sent-2411-405: The DLBacktrace framework is applicable in the following use cases:.
elements.sent-2411-406: Based on the global importance of sensitive features (e.g., gender, age, etc.) and their alignment with the data, it can be inferred whether the model or data has an undue bias towards any feature value.
elements.sent-2411-407: The layer-wise relevance can be used to accomplish this.
elements.sent-2411-408: The layers are generally multi-dimensional, for which proximity-based methods or white-box regression algorithms can derive outcomes.
elements.sent-2411-409: Future research on DLBacktrace will focus on expanding its applicability and improving its relevance scoring.
elements.sent-2411-410: Key areas include adapting it for complex models like advanced transformers and multimodal systems, ensuring its effectiveness across different AI applications.
elements.sent-2411-411: Efforts will also be made to reduce inference time for real-time use in production environments such as autonomous systems.
elements.sent-2411-412: We will explore it's usage for model optimization, including pruning by identifying non-essential components.
elements.sent-2411-413: Additionally, we will also explore it's usage for out-of-distribution (OOD) detection to enhance model robustness.
elements.sent-2411-414: DLBacktrace has the potential to support compliance with regulatory requirements, such as the EU GDPR Article 22, which grants individuals the right to an explanation regarding automated decision-making processes that significantly affect them.
elements.sent-2411-415: DLBacktrace helps ensure that AI systems remain accountable and meet these legal standards by providing transparent insights into model decisions.
elements.sent-2411-416: To foster responsible use, it is essential that DLBacktrace is implemented with the appropriate safeguards, including clear communication with users about how their data is being utilized and efforts to prevent any misuse.
elements.sent-2411-417: This study exclusively uses publicly available datasets and tools from open domains, adhering to their respective licensing and usage terms.
elements.sent-2411-418: No personal or sensitive data is involved in the study.
elements.sent-2411-419: Furthermore, the codebase used for this research is already publicly accessible, promoting transparency and reproducibility.. .
